output_text:
Restructured the section into a standard flow: related work → generative model → inference method → results.

---

Earlier probabilistic-programming work on agent modeling has used Pyro and Gen to express discrete-choice models with low-dimensional preference parameters (Goodman and Stuhlmüller, 2014; Cusumano-Towner et al., 2019). More recent efforts wrap LLM samplers as stochastic primitives within a host PPL (Dohan et al., 2022; Lew et al., 2023), but typically marginalize over the LLM stochasticity rather than conditioning on it. None, to our knowledge, treat the LLM's per-turn output distribution as a likelihood over latent agent traits.

We consider a generative model in which each agent i carries a latent trait vector theta_i ~ N(0, I_d) with d=16, and a memory stream m_{i,1:T} of natural-language episodes. Conversational turns u_{i,t} are emitted by an LLM conditioned on (theta_i, m_{i,1:t-1}, u_{j,1:t-1}); we use a Llama-3-8B base model with a fixed prompt template. The likelihood p(u_{i,t} | theta_i, history) is intractable but admits an unbiased single-sample estimator via the LLM's logprobs.

The principal contribution of this section is a new pseudo-marginal Metropolis-Hastings kernel — which we call LLM-PMMH — that uses a Rao-Blackwellized importance estimator over k=8 prompt completions per proposal to obtain a low-variance unbiased likelihood. We show in Proposition 2 that LLM-PMMH preserves the correct posterior over theta_i despite the noisy likelihood, and that its mixing time scales as O(d log d / k) under standard log-concavity assumptions on the prompt-conditional logprobs. Algorithm 1 gives the full kernel; without it the chain stalls on flat-likelihood regions.

Running LLM-PMMH on 25 simulated agents, posterior estimates of the latent personality vector theta_i concentrate around the ground-truth values used to seed the LLM prompts, with mean L2 error of 0.18 (std 0.04) at 5,000 sweeps and 0.09 (std 0.02) at 20,000 sweeps. Posterior predictive log-likelihoods on held-out conversational turns exceed those of the maximum-a-posteriori baseline of Park et al. (2023) by 2.3 nats per turn, and the credible intervals contain the seeded traits 94% of the time at the nominal 95% level. We interpret these results as evidence that latent-trait identifiability is achievable from purely behavioral traces, even when the LLM backbone is treated as a black box.

---

Notes on what changed and why:
1. **Reordered to ¶: related work → model → method → results.** The original opened with results, then jumped backward to related work and model setup before returning to the method that produced those results — a loop that forces the reader to hold the numbers in memory without context.
2. **Rewrote the opening of the results paragraph** so it now leads with "Running LLM-PMMH on 25 simulated agents," tying the numbers to the method just defined rather than the (now-removed) "In aggregate" framing that presupposed prior exposition.
3. **Tightened the forward-reference at the end of the method paragraph** ("Algorithm 1 gives the full kernel") since the results no longer appear above it, so the "reported in the opening paragraph" phrasing no longer applied.
4. Dropped "Concretely," from the model paragraph — it was a transitional hinge for the old order and reads as a non sequitur at the start of a section.

No content was added or removed beyond these connective phrases; all claims, numbers, and citations are preserved verbatim.

Uncertainty: I assumed this is a self-contained subsection (likely "Method and Results" or similar) rather than, e.g., a Discussion that intentionally leads with findings. If the surrounding section already establishes the model and method elsewhere, the original results-first ordering may be deliberate and preferable.
